+++ b/CLAUDE.md
@@ -0,0 +1,42 @@
+# Project Guidelines
+
+## Architecture
+- This project follows the repository pattern for data access
+- Services depend on repositories, never on each other directly
+- Events are preferred over direct cross-service calls for decoupling
+- All business logic lives in the service layer
+
+## Coding Conventions
+- Use TypeScript strict mode — no `any` types
+- All public methods must have JSDoc comments
+- Use dependency injection via constructor parameters
+- Error handling: throw typed errors, never return null for errors
+- Prefer async/await over raw Promises
+
+## Testing
+- Tests use vitest
+- Each module has a corresponding test file in tests/
+- Mock external dependencies, test business logic directly
+- Minimum: test the happy path and one error path per public method
+
+## File Organization
+- src/models/ — Data models and interfaces
+- src/repositories/ — Data access layer (implements repository interfaces)
+- src/services/ — Business logic layer
+- src/events/ — Event definitions and event bus
+- src/utils/ — Shared utilities (database, logger, pagination)
+- src/config/ — Configuration files
+- tests/ — Test files mirroring src/ structure
+
+## Git Practices
+- Commit atomically per logical change
+- Write descriptive commit messages explaining the "why"
+- Run tests before committing
+
+## Coordination
+- Read COORDINATION.md before starting any work
+- Update COORDINATION.md after completing your work with:
+  - What you did
+  - Key decisions and their rationale
+  - Warnings or context for the next agent
+  - Status of your assigned tasks

+# Coordination Log
+
+Use this file to share decisions, status updates, and context with other agents.
+Write your updates below. There is no enforced format — use whatever structure helps.
+
+---
+
+## Agent 1 — Analytics Dashboard API Design & Scaffolding
+
+### Status: COMPLETE
+
+### What I did
+1. **Created `src/models/analytics.ts`** — All TypeScript interfaces for the analytics feature:
+   - Query types: `AnalyticsQuery`, `TimeRange`, `MetricType`, `TimeGranularity`
+   - Core data types: `TimeSeriesPoint`, `MetricSummary`, `OrderStatusBreakdown`, `TopUser`
+   - Response types: `DashboardData`, `MetricDetailResponse`, `OrderStatusResponse`, `TopUsersResponse`
+
+2. **Created `src/services/analytics.service.ts`** — Scaffolded `AnalyticsService` with:
+   - Constructor injection of `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository`
+   - Public methods: `getDashboard()`, `getMetricDetail()`, `getOrderStatusBreakdown()`, `getTopUsers()`
+   - Private stubs with TODO comments: `getOrdersInRange()`, `computeMetric()`, `buildTimeSeries()`, `computeOrderStatusBreakdown()`, `computeTopUsers()`
+   - `getOrdersInRange()` already has basic date filtering implemented
+
+3. **Created `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`** — Framework-agnostic route handlers:
+   - `AnalyticsRouteHandler` class with methods for each endpoint
+   - `parseAnalyticsQuery()` and `parseMetricType()` validation helpers
+   - Request param types: `AnalyticsRequestParams`, `MetricDetailParams`, `TopUsersParams`
+
+4. **Updated `src/index.ts`** — Added all analytics exports
+
+5. **Created `DESIGN.md`** — Comprehensive design document
+
+### Key decisions
+- **No separate analytics repository**: Analytics are read-only aggregations over existing order/user data. No need for a separate store.
+- **Framework-agnostic route handlers**: Since the codebase has no HTTP framework, handlers accept typed params and return typed responses. Easy to wrap with Express/Fastify later.
+- **Response types include the query**: Self-describing for debugging and frontend caching.
+- **Previous-period comparison built into MetricSummary**: Enables trend arrows without extra API calls.
+
+### Route structure
+```
+GET /api/analytics/dashboard         — Full dashboard
+GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type     — Single metric (revenue|orders|users|conversion)
+GET /api/analytics/orders/status     — Order status distribution
+GET /api/analytics/users/top         — Top users by revenue
+```
+
+### For the next agent
+- **All code compiles cleanly** (`tsc --noEmit` passes).
+- **The service methods are stubs** returning placeholder data (zeroes/empty arrays). The private methods have detailed TODO comments explaining what to implement.
+- **Start with `computeMetric()`** in `analytics.service.ts` — it's the core aggregation logic. Then `buildTimeSeries()`, `computeOrderStatusBreakdown()`, and `computeTopUsers()`.
+- **Tests should go in** `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts` and `tests/routes/analytics.routes.test.ts`.
+- Read `DESIGN.md` for the full design rationale and implementation guide.

diff --git a/DESIGN.md b/DESIGN.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..66847c0
--- /dev/null
+++ b/DESIGN.md
@@ -0,0 +1,135 @@
+# Analytics Dashboard — Design Document
+
+## Overview
+
+This document describes the design of the analytics dashboard feature for TaskFlow Pro. The feature provides aggregated metrics, order breakdowns, and user leaderboards computed from existing order and user data.
+
+## Route Structure
+
+```
+GET /api/analytics/dashboard         — Full dashboard (all metrics + breakdowns)
+GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type     — Single metric detail (revenue|orders|users|conversion)
+GET /api/analytics/orders/status     — Order status distribution
+GET /api/analytics/users/top         — Top users by revenue
+```
+
+### Why this structure
+
+- **`/dashboard`** is the primary endpoint, returning everything the frontend needs in one call to minimize round trips.
+- **`/metrics/:type`** allows the frontend to fetch individual metrics on-demand (e.g., when a user clicks into a metric card) without re-fetching the full dashboard.
+- **`/orders/status`** and **`/users/top`** are separated because they serve distinct UI panels that may refresh independently or have different caching characteristics.
+
+### Common query parameters
+
+All endpoints accept:
+- `start` (ISO 8601, required) — beginning of the time range
+- `end` (ISO 8601, required) — end of the time range
+- `granularity` (optional, default `"day"`) — bucket size for time-series: `hour | day | week | month`
+- `/users/top` also accepts `limit` (optional, default `10`)
+
+## Data Model Design
+
+### Core types (`src/models/analytics.ts`)
+
+| Type | Purpose |
+|------|---------|
+| `AnalyticsQuery` | Input to all service methods — encapsulates time range, granularity, and optional filters |
+| `TimeSeriesPoint` | Single data point with timestamp, value, and label — used in all time-series charts |
+| `MetricSummary` | One metric's current value, previous-period value, trend direction, and time-series |
+| `OrderStatusBreakdown` | Count + percentage for a single order status |
+| `TopUser` | A user row with order count and revenue total |
+
+### Response wrappers
+
+Each route returns a response type that wraps the core data with the original query and a `generatedAt` timestamp. This makes responses self-describing and helps with debugging and caching.
+
+- `DashboardData` — contains `MetricSummary[]`, `OrderStatusBreakdown[]`, `TopUser[]`
+- `MetricDetailResponse` — contains one `MetricSummary`
+- `OrderStatusResponse` — contains `OrderStatusBreakdown[]` + `totalOrders`
+- `TopUsersResponse` — contains `TopUser[]` + `limit`
+
+### Why explicit query in responses
+
+Including the `AnalyticsQuery` in every response means consumers can verify what time range produced the data, which is critical when multiple dashboard panels update independently.
+
+## Aggregation Service Design (`src/services/analytics.service.ts`)
+
+### Architecture
+
+```
+Route Handler (parses HTTP params)
+    → AnalyticsService (aggregation logic)
+        → OrderRepository (read orders)
+        → UserRepository (read users)
+```
+
+The AnalyticsService follows the same patterns as existing services:
+- **Constructor injection** of OrderRepository and UserRepository
+- **No cross-service dependencies** — reads directly from repositories
+- **Async methods** for consistency, even though current aggregation is synchronous (future caching or DB queries will be async)
+
+### Implementation guide for the next developer
+
+The service is scaffolded with placeholder methods. Here's what needs to be implemented:
+
+1. **`computeMetric(type, orders, users, query)`** — The core aggregation method. Switch on metric type:
+   - `revenue`: Sum `order.total` for all orders in range
+   - `orders`: Count of orders
+   - `users`: Count of distinct `order.userId` values
+   - `conversion`: `orders / users` ratio (handle division by zero)
+   - For previous-period comparison: shift the time range back by its own duration and compute the same metric
+
+2. **`buildTimeSeries(query)`** — Bucket the time range by granularity:
+   - Generate bucket boundaries from `start` to `end` using the granularity
+   - For each bucket, aggregate the relevant value
+   - Generate labels (e.g., `"Mon"` for day, `"Jan"` for month)
+
+3. **`computeOrderStatusBreakdown(orders)`** — Group by `order.status`, count each group, compute percentages
+
+4. **`computeTopUsers(orders, users, limit)`** — Group orders by `userId`, sum revenue per user, join with user names, sort descending, slice to limit
+
+### Performance considerations
+
+- Current approach: compute all analytics on-the-fly by reading all matching records. This is fine for the in-memory database with moderate data volumes.
+- Future optimization: if data grows, consider a materialized analytics repository that pre-aggregates data on write (listen to `order:created` / `order:status-changed` events).
+
+## Route Handler Design (`src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`)
+
+The route handlers are framework-agnostic:
+- They accept typed request parameter objects (not `req`/`res`)
+- They return typed response objects (not calling `res.json()`)
+- When integrating with Express/Fastify/etc., wrap each method in the framework's route registration
+
+This keeps business logic testable without spinning up an HTTP server.
+
+### Validation
+
+`parseAnalyticsQuery()` and `parseMetricType()` handle input validation and throw descriptive errors. The HTTP integration layer should catch these and return 400 responses.
+
+## File Organization
+
+```
+src/
+  models/analytics.ts              — All analytics interfaces and types
+  services/analytics.service.ts    — Aggregation business logic (stub)
+  routes/analytics.routes.ts       — Route handlers + param parsing
+  index.ts                         — Updated with analytics exports
+```
+
+## Decisions Log
+
+| Decision | Rationale |
+|----------|-----------|
+| No separate analytics repository | Analytics are read-only aggregations over existing data; no need for a separate data store yet |
+| Framework-agnostic route handlers | The codebase has no HTTP framework; this keeps things flexible |
+| Time range as explicit dates | Deterministic + testable; the frontend converts "last 7 days" to concrete dates |
+| Previous-period comparison in MetricSummary | Most dashboards show trend arrows; computing this server-side avoids two API calls |
+| All response types include the query | Self-describing responses simplify debugging and frontend caching |
+| Async service methods | Future-proofs for when aggregation hits a real database |
+
+## What's Next
+
+- **Agent 2/3**: Implement the aggregation logic in `AnalyticsService` private methods
+- **Agent 3/4**: Write tests for the service and route handlers
+- **Agent 4/5**: Add event listeners to optionally pre-compute analytics on order events
+- Consider adding caching at the service level if aggregation becomes expensive

diff --git a/src/services/analytics.service.ts b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
new file mode 100644

+++ b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,243 @@
+/**
+ * Analytics service — aggregation logic for the analytics dashboard.
+ *
+ * Depends on:
+ * - OrderRepository (data layer) for order data
+ * - UserRepository (data layer) for user data
+ *
+ * Architecture notes:
+ * - This service reads from existing repositories; it does NOT maintain
+ *   its own data store. All analytics are computed on-the-fly from
+ *   order and user data.
+ * - The service does not depend on other services (per CLAUDE.md guidelines).
+ * - Each public method maps to one API route (see analytics.routes.ts).
+ * - Methods accept an AnalyticsQuery and return fully-formed response types.
+ *
+ * Implementation guide for the next developer:
+ * - Replace the placeholder returns with real aggregation logic.
+ * - Use OrderRepository.findAll() with date predicates to filter by time range.
+ * - Use the TimeGranularity to bucket time-series data (see buildTimeSeries stub).
+ * - Consider caching if aggregation becomes expensive.
+ */
+
+import type { OrderRepository } from '../repositories/order.repository.js';
+import type { UserRepository } from '../repositories/user.repository.js';
+import type {
+  AnalyticsQuery,
+  DashboardData,
+  MetricDetailResponse,
+  MetricSummary,
+  MetricType,
+  OrderStatusBreakdown,
+  OrderStatusResponse,
+  TimeSeriesPoint,
+  TopUser,
+  TopUsersResponse,
+} from '../models/analytics.js';
+import type { Order } from '../models/order.js';
+import type { User } from '../models/user.js';
+import { Logger } from '../utils/logger.js';
+
+/** Default number of top users to include in leaderboards. */
+const DEFAULT_TOP_USERS_LIMIT = 10;
+
+export class AnalyticsService {
+  private logger = new Logger('AnalyticsService');
+
+  constructor(
+    private orderRepository: OrderRepository,
+    private userRepository: UserRepository,
+  ) {}
+
+  // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+  // Public API — one method per route
+  // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+  /**
+   * Get the full dashboard dataset including all metric summaries,
+   * order status breakdown, and top users.
+   *
+   * Route: GET /api/analytics/dashboard
+   */
+  async getDashboard(query: AnalyticsQuery): Promise<DashboardData> {
+    this.logger.info('Generating dashboard data');
+
+    const orders = this.getOrdersInRange(query);
+    const users = this.userRepository.findAll();
+
+    const metrics: MetricSummary[] = [
+      this.computeMetric('revenue', orders, users, query),
+      this.computeMetric('orders', orders, users, query),
+      this.computeMetric('users', orders, users, query),
+      this.computeMetric('conversion', orders, users, query),
+    ];
+
+    return {
+      query,
+      metrics,
+      orderStatusBreakdown: this.computeOrderStatusBreakdown(orders),
+      topUsers: this.computeTopUsers(orders, users, DEFAULT_TOP_USERS_LIMIT),
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get detailed data for a single metric type.
+   *
+   * Route: GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type
+   */
+  async getMetricDetail(
+    metricType: MetricType,
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+  ): Promise<MetricDetailResponse> {
+    this.logger.info(`Generating metric detail for: ${metricType}`);
+
+    const orders = this.getOrdersInRange(query);
+    const users = this.userRepository.findAll();
+
+    return {
+      query: { ...query, metricType },
+      metric: this.computeMetric(metricType, orders, users, query),
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get order status breakdown for the given time range.
+   *
+   * Route: GET /api/analytics/orders/status
+   */
+  async getOrderStatusBreakdown(
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+  ): Promise<OrderStatusResponse> {
+    this.logger.info('Generating order status breakdown');
+
+    const orders = this.getOrdersInRange(query);
+    const breakdown = this.computeOrderStatusBreakdown(orders);
+
+    return {
+      query,
+      breakdown,
+      totalOrders: orders.length,
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get the top users by revenue for the given time range.
+   *
+   * Route: GET /api/analytics/users/top
+   */
+  async getTopUsers(
+    query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    limit: number = DEFAULT_TOP_USERS_LIMIT,
+  ): Promise<TopUsersResponse> {
+    this.logger.info(`Generating top ${limit} users`);
+
+    const orders = this.getOrdersInRange(query);
+    const users = this.userRepository.findAll();
+
+    return {
+      query,
+      users: this.computeTopUsers(orders, users, limit),
+      limit,
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+  // Private helpers — implement aggregation logic here
+  // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+  /**
+   * Filter orders to those within the query's time range.
+   *
+   * TODO (Agent 2/3): Implement proper date filtering using query.timeRange.
+   * For now returns all orders as a placeholder.
+   */
+  private getOrdersInRange(query: AnalyticsQuery): Order[] {
+    this.logger.debug(
+      `Filtering orders for range: ${query.timeRange.start.toISOString()} - ${query.timeRange.end.toISOString()}`,
+    );
+    return this.orderRepository.findAll((order) => {
+      return (
+        order.createdAt >= query.timeRange.start &&
+        order.createdAt <= query.timeRange.end
+      );
+    });
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Compute a MetricSummary for the given metric type.
+   *
+   * TODO (Agent 2/3): Implement real aggregation per metric type.
+   * - revenue: sum of order.total
+   * - orders: count of orders
+   * - users: count of distinct userIds in orders
+   * - conversion: orders / users ratio
+   *
+   * Also implement previous-period comparison by shifting the time range
+   * back by its own duration and computing the same metric.
+   */
+  private computeMetric(
+    type: MetricType,
+    _orders: Order[],
+    _users: User[],
+    _query: AnalyticsQuery,
+  ): MetricSummary {
+    // Placeholder — return zeroed metric with empty time series
+    return {
+      type,
+      currentValue: 0,
+      previousValue: 0,
+      changePercent: 0,
+      trend: 'flat',
+      timeSeries: this.buildTimeSeries(_query),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Build time-series data points for the query's time range and granularity.
+   *
+   * TODO (Agent 2/3): Implement bucketing logic.
+   * - Divide the time range into buckets based on query.granularity
+   * - For each bucket, compute the aggregate value
+   * - Generate human-readable labels (e.g., "Mon", "Jan 5")
+   */
+  private buildTimeSeries(_query: AnalyticsQuery): TimeSeriesPoint[] {
+    // Placeholder — return empty series
+    return [];
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Compute the breakdown of orders by status.
+   *
+   * TODO (Agent 2/3): Implement by grouping orders by status and
+   * calculating count + percentage.
+   */
+  private computeOrderStatusBreakdown(
+    _orders: Order[],
+  ): OrderStatusBreakdown[] {
+    // Placeholder — return empty breakdown
+    return [];
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Compute the top users ranked by total revenue.
+   *
+   * TODO (Agent 2/3): Implement by:
+   * 1. Grouping orders by userId
+   * 2. Summing revenue per user
+   * 3. Looking up user names from UserRepository
+   * 4. Sorting by revenue descending
+   * 5. Slicing to the requested limit
+   */
+  private computeTopUsers(
+    _orders: Order[],
+    _users: User[],
+    _limit: number,
+  ): TopUser[] {
+    // Placeholder — return empty list
+    return [];
+  }
+}

parameters.content:
# Analytics Dashboard — Design Document

## Overview

This document describes the design of the analytics dashboard feature for TaskFlow Pro. The feature provides aggregated metrics, order breakdowns, and user leaderboards computed from existing order and user data.

## Route Structure

```
GET /api/analytics/dashboard         — Full dashboard (all metrics + breakdowns)
GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type     — Single metric detail (revenue|orders|users|conversion)
GET /api/analytics/orders/status     — Order status distribution
GET /api/analytics/users/top         — Top users by revenue
```

### Why this structure

- **`/dashboard`** is the primary endpoint, returning everything the frontend needs in one call to minimize round trips.
- **`/metrics/:type`** allows the frontend to fetch individual metrics on-demand (e.g., when a user clicks into a metric card) without re-fetching the full dashboard.
- **`/orders/status`** and **`/users/top`** are separated because they serve distinct UI panels that may refresh independently or have different caching characteristics.

### Common query parameters

All endpoints accept:
- `start` (ISO 8601, required) — beginning of the time range
- `end` (ISO 8601, required) — end of the time range
- `granularity` (optional, default `"day"`) — bucket size for time-series: `hour | day | week | month`
- `/users/top` also accepts `limit` (optional, default `10`)

## Data Model Design

### Core types (`src/models/analytics.ts`)

| Type | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `AnalyticsQuery` | Input to all service methods — encapsulates time range, granularity, and optional filters |
| `TimeSeriesPoint` | Single data point with timestamp, value, and label — used in all time-series charts |
| `MetricSummary` | One metric's current value, previous-period value, trend direction, and time-series |
| `OrderStatusBreakdown` | Count + percentage for a single order status |
| `TopUser` | A user row with order count and revenue total |

### Response wrappers

Each route returns a response type that wraps the core data with the original query and a `generatedAt` timestamp. This makes responses self-describing and helps with debugging and caching.

- `DashboardData` — contains `MetricSummary[]`, `OrderStatusBreakdown[]`, `TopUser[]`
- `MetricDetailResponse` — contains one `MetricSummary`
- `OrderStatusResponse` — contains `OrderStatusBreakdown[]` + `totalOrders`
- `TopUsersResponse` — contains `TopUser[]` + `limit`

### Why explicit query in responses

Including the `AnalyticsQuery` in every response means consumers can verify what time range produced the data, which is critical when multiple dashboard panels update independently.

## Aggregation Service Design (`src/services/analytics.service.ts`)

### Architecture

```
Route Handler (parses HTTP params)
    → AnalyticsService (aggregation logic)
        → OrderRepository (read orders)
        → UserRepository (read users)
```

The AnalyticsService follows the same patterns as existing services:
- **Constructor injection** of OrderRepository and UserRepository
- **No cross-service dependencies** — reads directly from repositories
- **Async methods** for consistency, even though current aggregation is synchronous (future caching or DB queries will be async)

### Implementation guide for the next developer

The service is scaffolded with placeholder methods. Here's what needs to be implemented:

1. **`computeMetric(type, orders, users, query)`** — The core aggregation method. Switch on metric type:
   - `revenue`: Sum `order.total` for all orders in range
   - `orders`: Count of orders
   - `users`: Count of distinct `order.userId` values
   - `conversion`: `orders / users` ratio (handle division by zero)
   - For previous-period comparison: shift the time range back by its own duration and compute the same metric

2. **`buildTimeSeries(query)`** — Bucket the time range by granularity:
   - Generate bucket boundaries from `start` to `end` using the granularity
   - For each bucket, aggregate the relevant value
   - Generate labels (e.g., `"Mon"` for day, `"Jan"` for month)

3. **`computeOrderStatusBreakdown(orders)`** — Group by `order.status`, count each group, compute percentages

4. **`computeTopUsers(orders, users, limit)`** — Group orders by `userId`, sum revenue per user, join with user names, sort descending, slice to limit

### Performance considerations

- Current approach: compute all analytics on-the-fly by reading all matching records. This is fine for the in-memory database with moderate data volumes.
- Future optimization: if data grows, consider a materialized analytics repository that pre-aggregates data on write (listen to `order:created` / `order:status-changed` events).

## Route Handler Design (`src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`)

The route handlers are framework-agnostic:
- They accept typed request parameter objects (not `req`/`res`)
- They return typed response objects (not calling `res.json()`)
- When integrating with Express/Fastify/etc., wrap each method in the framework's route registration

This keeps business logic testable without spinning up an HTTP server.

### Validation

`parseAnalyticsQuery()` and `parseMetricType()` handle input validation and throw descriptive errors. The HTTP integration layer should catch these and return 400 responses.

## File Organization

```
src/
  models/analytics.ts              — All analytics interfaces and types
  services/analytics.service.ts    — Aggregation business logic (stub)
  routes/analytics.routes.ts       — Route handlers + param parsing
  index.ts                         — Updated with analytics exports
```

## Decisions Log

| Decision | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|
| No separate analytics repository | Analytics are read-only aggregations over existing data; no need for a separate data store yet |
| Framework-agnostic route handlers | The codebase has no HTTP framework; this keeps things flexible |
| Time range as explicit dates | Deterministic + testable; the frontend converts "last 7 days" to concrete dates |
| Previous-period comparison in MetricSummary | Most dashboards show trend arrows; computing this server-side avoids two API calls |
| All response types include the query | Self-describing responses simplify debugging and frontend caching |
| Async service methods | Future-proofs for when aggregation hits a real database |

## What's Next

- **Agent 2/3**: Implement the aggregation logic in `AnalyticsService` private methods
- **Agent 3/4**: Write tests for the service and route handlers
- **Agent 4/5**: Add event listeners to optionally pre-compute analytics on order events
- Consider adding caching at the service level if aggregation becomes expensive

### Status: COMPLETE

### What I did

   - Core data types: `TimeSeriesPoint`, `MetricSummary`, `OrderStatusBreakdown`, `TopUser`
   - Response types: `DashboardData`, `MetricDetailResponse`, `OrderStatusResponse`, `TopUsersResponse`

2. **Created `src/services/analytics.service.ts`** — Scaffolded `AnalyticsService` with:
   - Constructor injection of `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository`
   - Public methods: `getDashboard()`, `getMetricDetail()`, `getOrderStatusBreakdown()`, `getTopUsers()`
   - Private stubs with TODO comments: `getOrdersInRange()`, `computeMetric()`, `buildTimeSeries()`, `computeOrderStatusBreakdown()`, `computeTopUsers()`
   - `getOrdersInRange()` already has basic date filtering implemented

3. **Created `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`** — Framework-agnostic route handlers:
   - `AnalyticsRouteHandler` class with methods for each endpoint
   - `parseAnalyticsQuery()` and `parseMetricType()` validation helpers
   - Request param types: `AnalyticsRequestParams`, `MetricDetailParams`, `TopUsersParams`

4. **Updated `src/index.ts`** — Added all analytics exports

5. **Created `DESIGN.md`** — Comprehensive design document

### Key decisions
- **No separate analytics repository**: Analytics are read-only aggregations over existing order/user data. No need for a separate store.
- **Framework-agnostic route handlers**: Since the codebase has no HTTP framework, handlers accept typed params and return typed responses. Easy to wrap with Express/Fastify later.
- **Response types include the query**: Self-describing for debugging and frontend caching.
- **Previous-period comparison built into MetricSummary**: Enables trend arrows without extra API calls.

### Route structure
```
GET /api/analytics/dashboard         — Full dashboard
GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type     — Single metric (revenue|orders|users|conversion)
GET /api/analytics/orders/status     — Order status distribution
GET /api/analytics/users/top         — Top users by revenue
```
